Electron Spin

What you will learn

  • Magnetism arises from the circular motion of charged particles.

  • Atoms with electrons that have nonzero orbital angular momentum generate magnetic moments, making them susceptible to the effects of an external magnetic field.

  • The Stern-Gerlach experiment established the existence of an intrinsic magnetic moment in electrons, called spin, which is affected by a magnetic field independently of orbital angular momentum.

  • Despite the name, spin is an intrinsic magnetic property of subatomic particles and does not involve literal “spinning” or motion. Spin is a fundamental property of particles, akin to mass or charge.

  • Particles with half-integer spins (e.g., 1/21/21/2, 3/23/23/2, 5/25/25/2) are classified as fermions, while those with integer spins (e.g., 0, 1, 2) are classified as bosons. These two families follow different rules. Fermions obey the Pauli exclusion principle, which states that no two identical fermions can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously. In contrast, bosons are not restricted in this way and can occupy the same state, allowing them to “bunch together.”

  • Normal Zeeman effect: the splitting of singlet states (spin zero) in a magnetic field due to the electron’s orbital angular momentum, which can be explained classically.

  • Anomalous Zeeman effect: a more general case where both electron spin and orbital angular momentum contribute to the splitting of energy levels in a magnetic field.

Rotating charge generates a magnetic moment¶

  • A moving charge generates a magnetic moment, which interacts with an external magnetic field.

  • When an electron is in a state with l>0l > 0l>0, one can picture a circular motion of charge (the wavefunction describing the electron) around the nucleus, generating its own magnetic field.

  • Note that this motion is not classical; here we are just building a wireframe picture based on a classical interpretation.

Magnetic moment of the electron¶

  • According to classical mechanics, a charged particle like an electron rotating around an axis has a magnetic moment given by:

μ⃗=γeL⃗{\vec{\mu} = \gamma_e\vec{L}}μ​=γe​L
(1)

  • where γe\gamma_eγe​ is the magnetogyric ratio of the electron, expressed via fundamental constants as −e2me-\frac{e}{2m_e}−2me​e​. We choose the external magnetic field to lie along the zzz-axis, so it is the zzz component of μ⃗\vec{\mu}μ​ that matters:

μz=−(e2me)Lz=−(eℏ2me)m≡−μBm{\mu_z = -\left(\frac{e}{2m_e}\right)L_z = -\left(\frac{e\hbar}{2m_e}\right)m \equiv -\mu_B m}μz​=−(2me​e​)Lz​=−(2me​eℏ​)m≡−μB​m
(2)

  • where μB\mu_BμB​ is the Bohr magneton. The interaction between a magnetic moment and an external magnetic field is given by the classical expression:

U=−μ⃗⋅B⃗=−∣μ⃗∣∣B⃗∣cos⁡(α){U = -\vec{\mu}\cdot\vec{B} = -|\vec{\mu}||\vec{B}|\cos(\alpha)}U=−μ​⋅B=−∣μ​∣∣B∣cos(α)
(3)

  • where α\alphaα is the angle between the two vectors. This gives the energy for a bar magnet in the presence of an external field.

  • Unlike in quantum mechanics, in classical mechanics any orientation is allowed. When the external field is oriented along the zzz-axis, the classical potential energy of interaction with the field is:

U=−μzB=eB2meLz{U = -\mu_z B = \frac{eB}{2m_e}L_z}U=−μz​B=2me​eB​Lz​
(4)

  • In quantum mechanics, to get the Hamiltonian term for interaction with the magnetic field, we simply replace the classical angular momentum with the corresponding operator:

H^mag=eB2meL^z\hat{H}_{mag} = \frac{eB}{2m_e}\hat{L}_zH^mag​=2me​eB​L^z​
(5)

Effect of a magnetic field on atoms¶

  • In quantum mechanics, a magnetic moment (here corresponding to a non-sss orbital electron) may only take specific orientations.

  • The zzz-axis is often called the quantization axis. The eigenvalues of L^z\hat{L}_zL^z​ give the possible orientations of the magnetic moment with respect to the external field.

  • For example, consider an electron in a 2p2p2p orbital of a hydrogenlike atom. The electron may reside in any of 2p+12p_{+1}2p+1​, 2p02p_02p0​, or 2p−12p_{-1}2p−1​ (degenerate without the field). For these orbitals LzL_zLz​ may take the values +ℏ+\hbar+ℏ, 0, −ℏ-\hbar−ℏ:

L^z∣p+1⟩=+1×ℏ∣p+1⟩{\hat{L}_z|p_{+1}\rangle = +1\times\hbar|p_{+1}\rangle}L^z​∣p+1​⟩=+1×ℏ∣p+1​⟩
(6)
L^z∣p0⟩=0×ℏ∣p0⟩{\hat{L}_z|p_0\rangle = 0\times\hbar|p_{0}\rangle}L^z​∣p0​⟩=0×ℏ∣p0​⟩
(7)
L^z∣p−1⟩=−1×ℏ∣p−1⟩{\hat{L}_z|p_{-1}\rangle = -1\times\hbar|p_{-1}\rangle}L^z​∣p−1​⟩=−1×ℏ∣p−1​⟩
(8)

A magnetic field modifies the Hamiltonian¶

  • The total quantum mechanical Hamiltonian for a hydrogenlike atom in a magnetic field can be written as:

H^=H^0+eB2meL^z{\hat{H} = \hat{H}_0 + \frac{eB}{2m_e}\hat{L}_z}H^=H^0​+2me​eB​L^z​
(9)

  • where H^0\hat{H}_0H^0​ denotes the Hamiltonian in the absence of the magnetic field.

  • Since the projection of angular momentum commutes with the Hamiltonian, [L^z,H^0]=0[\hat{L}_z, \hat{H}_0] = 0[L^z​,H^0​]=0, both operators share the same eigenfunctions:

L^z∣n,l,ml⟩=ℏml∣n,l,ml⟩\hat{L}_z \mid n,l, m_l\rangle = \hbar m_l \mid n,l, m_l\rangleL^z​∣n,l,ml​⟩=ℏml​∣n,l,ml​⟩
(10)
H^0∣n,l,ml⟩=En∣n,l,ml⟩\hat{H}_0 \mid n,l, m_l\rangle = E_n \mid n,l, m_l\rangleH^0​∣n,l,ml​⟩=En​∣n,l,ml​⟩
(11)
H-atom in a magnetic field
Enlm=En+μBmlB{E_{nlm} = E_n + \mu_B m_l B}Enlm​=En​+μB​ml​B
(12)

  • n=1,2,...n = 1, 2, ...n=1,2,... determines the energy levels EnE_nEn​ in the absence of a magnetic field.

  • μB=ℏe2me\mu_B = \frac{\hbar e}{2 m_e}μB​=2me​ℏe​ is the constant we call the Bohr magneton.

  • ml=−l,...,0,...,+lm_l = -l, ..., 0, ..., +lml​=−l,...,0,...,+l.

Zeeman effect¶

  • We see that the magnetic field now enters the expression for the energy levels.

  • The field splits the (2l+1)(2l + 1)(2l+1) degenerate levels. This is called the orbital Zeeman effect.

  • For instance, the 2p2p2p level of the hydrogen atom splits into 3 levels at energies −μBB-\mu_B B−μB​B, 0, +μBB+\mu_B B+μB​B.

Discovering spin¶

Spin as a tiny magnet¶

  • We come to view the spin of subatomic particles in the same way as mass and charge. Spin is an intrinsic property of subatomic particles like protons, electrons, and photons.

  • Spin manifests itself via a permanent magnetic moment. A particle with spin can interact with magnetic fields just as a charged particle interacts with electric fields. This is why spin is pictorially depicted as a tiny magnet.

  • The Schrödinger equation does not naturally account for electron spin. This is because spin lacks a classical analogue, so our approach of taking the classical Hamiltonian and replacing terms with operators does not work directly for spin.

  • The concept of electron spin originates from the Dirac equation, which is essentially a generalization of the Schrödinger equation to include relativistic effects.

  • Fortunately, spin can be incorporated into the Schrödinger equation once we recognize its existence. We simply add an additional quantum number, sss, which behaves similarly to the orbital angular momentum lll.

Quantum numbers and eigenfunctions of spin¶

  • Spin and its projection, SSS and SzS_zSz​, have the same properties as the angular momentum LLL and LzL_zLz​. Quantum mechanics requires the number of allowed spin projections to be:

2s+12s + 12s+1
(13)

  • Experiments show that we only observe two values of spin:

2s+1=2⇒s=122s + 1 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{1}{2}2s+1=2⇒s=21​
(14)

  • Projections are determined by the quantum number msm_sms​, an analog of mlm_lml​:

ms=−s,+s=−1/2,+1/2m_s = -s, +s = -1/2, +1/2ms​=−s,+s=−1/2,+1/2
(15)

  • We expect two eigenfunctions for the two outcomes and denote the eigenfunctions of SzS_zSz​ as α\alphaα and β\betaβ:

Eigenfunctions of spin, α\alphaα and β\betaβ

  • Spin is an intrinsic angular momentum with a fixed eigenvalue:

S=32ℏS = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\hbarS=23​​ℏ
(16)
Sz=±12ℏS_z = \pm \frac{1}{2}\hbarSz​=±21​ℏ
(17)

  • Spin eigenfunctions and eigenvalues:

S^2∣α⟩=ℏ2s(s+1)∣α⟩=34ℏ2∣α⟩\hat{S}^2 |\alpha\rangle = \hbar^2 s(s+1) |\alpha\rangle = \frac{3}{4} \hbar^2 |\alpha\rangleS^2∣α⟩=ℏ2s(s+1)∣α⟩=43​ℏ2∣α⟩
(18)
S^2∣β⟩=ℏ2s(s+1)∣β⟩=34ℏ2∣β⟩\hat{S}^2 |\beta\rangle = \hbar^2 s(s+1) |\beta\rangle = \frac{3}{4} \hbar^2 |\beta\rangleS^2∣β⟩=ℏ2s(s+1)∣β⟩=43​ℏ2∣β⟩
(19)
S^z∣α⟩=+12ℏ∣α⟩\hat{S}_z |\alpha\rangle = +\frac{1}{2} \hbar |\alpha\rangleS^z​∣α⟩=+21​ℏ∣α⟩
(20)
S^z∣β⟩=−12ℏ∣β⟩\hat{S}_z |\beta\rangle = -\frac{1}{2} \hbar |\beta\rangleS^z​∣β⟩=−21​ℏ∣β⟩
(21)

Orthogonality and normalization of the spin part of the wavefunction¶

  • Note that the following operators commute: H^\hat{H}H^, L^2\hat{L}^2L^2, L^z\hat{L}_zL^z​, S^2\hat{S}^2S^2, and S^z\hat{S}_zS^z​. This implies that all these quantities can be specified simultaneously.

  • The spin eigenfunctions α\alphaα and β\betaβ are, as expected, orthonormal:

∫α∗α dσ≡⟨α∣α⟩=∫β∗β dσ≡⟨β∣β⟩=1\int \alpha^* \alpha \, d\sigma \equiv \langle \alpha | \alpha \rangle = \int \beta^* \beta \, d\sigma \equiv \langle \beta | \beta \rangle = 1∫α∗αdσ≡⟨α∣α⟩=∫β∗βdσ≡⟨β∣β⟩=1
(22)
∫α∗β dσ≡⟨α∣β⟩=∫β∗α dσ≡⟨β∣α⟩=0\int \alpha^* \beta \, d\sigma \equiv \langle \alpha | \beta \rangle = \int \beta^* \alpha \, d\sigma \equiv \langle \beta | \alpha \rangle = 0∫α∗βdσ≡⟨α∣β⟩=∫β∗αdσ≡⟨β∣α⟩=0
(23)

  • To completely describe a hydrogenlike atom, the wavefunction must include the spin component. Thus the total wavefunction is a product of the spatial wavefunction and the spin part:

∣n,l,ml,ms⟩=ψn,l,ml(r,θ,ϕ) σ(s),where σ=α or β|n,l, m_l, m_s\rangle = \psi_{n, l, m_l}(r, \theta, \phi)\,\sigma(s), \quad \text{where } \sigma = \alpha \text{ or } \beta∣n,l,ml​,ms​⟩=ψn,l,ml​​(r,θ,ϕ)σ(s),where σ=α or β
(24)

  • Wavefunctions differing in their spin part are orthogonal:

⟨1,0,0,+1/2∣1,0,0,−1/2⟩=0\langle 1, 0, 0, +1/2 \mid 1, 0, 0, -1/2\rangle = 0⟨1,0,0,+1/2∣1,0,0,−1/2⟩=0
(25)
Action of spin operators on eigenstates

In quantum mechanics the spin of an electron is represented by the spin operator S⃗\vec{S}S, with components SxS_xSx​, SyS_ySy​, and SzS_zSz​. For spin-12\frac{1}{2}21​ particles, these operators are represented by the Pauli matrices σx\sigma_xσx​, σy\sigma_yσy​, and σz\sigma_zσz​ (multiplied by ℏ2\frac{\hbar}{2}2ℏ​):

Sx=ℏ2σx,Sy=ℏ2σy,Sz=ℏ2σzS_x = \frac{\hbar}{2} \sigma_x, \quad S_y = \frac{\hbar}{2} \sigma_y, \quad S_z = \frac{\hbar}{2} \sigma_zSx​=2ℏ​σx​,Sy​=2ℏ​σy​,Sz​=2ℏ​σz​
(26)

The Pauli matrices are:

σx=(0110),σy=(0−ii0),σz=(100−1)\sigma_x = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \sigma_y = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -i \\ i & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \sigma_z = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}σx​=(01​10​),σy​=(0i​−i0​),σz​=(10​0−1​)
(27)

The spin eigenstates α\alphaα and β\betaβ correspond to “spin-up” and “spin-down” along the zzz-axis:

α=(10),β=(01)\alpha = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \beta = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}α=(10​),β=(01​)
(28)

  1. SzS_zSz​ operator:

Szα=ℏ2(100−1)(10)=ℏ2αS_z \alpha = \frac{\hbar}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{\hbar}{2} \alphaSz​α=2ℏ​(10​0−1​)(10​)=2ℏ​α
(29)
Szβ=ℏ2(100−1)(01)=−ℏ2βS_z \beta = \frac{\hbar}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = -\frac{\hbar}{2} \betaSz​β=2ℏ​(10​0−1​)(01​)=−2ℏ​β
(30)

  1. SxS_xSx​ operator:

Sxα=ℏ2(0110)(10)=ℏ2βS_x \alpha = \frac{\hbar}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{\hbar}{2} \betaSx​α=2ℏ​(01​10​)(10​)=2ℏ​β
(31)
Sxβ=ℏ2(0110)(01)=ℏ2αS_x \beta = \frac{\hbar}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{\hbar}{2} \alphaSx​β=2ℏ​(01​10​)(01​)=2ℏ​α
(32)

  1. SyS_ySy​ operator:

Syα=ℏ2(0−ii0)(10)=iℏ2βS_y \alpha = \frac{\hbar}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -i \\ i & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{i \hbar}{2} \betaSy​α=2ℏ​(0i​−i0​)(10​)=2iℏ​β
(33)
Syβ=ℏ2(0−ii0)(01)=−iℏ2αS_y \beta = \frac{\hbar}{2} \begin{pmatrix} 0 & -i \\ i & 0 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = -\frac{i \hbar}{2} \alphaSy​β=2ℏ​(0i​−i0​)(01​)=−2iℏ​α
(34)

Summary table

Operatorα\alphaα (spin-up)β\betaβ (spin-down)
SzS_zSz​ℏ2α\frac{\hbar}{2} \alpha2ℏ​α−ℏ2β-\frac{\hbar}{2} \beta−2ℏ​β
SxS_xSx​ℏ2β\frac{\hbar}{2} \beta2ℏ​βℏ2α\frac{\hbar}{2} \alpha2ℏ​α
SyS_ySy​iℏ2β\frac{i \hbar}{2} \beta2iℏ​β−iℏ2α-\frac{i \hbar}{2} \alpha−2iℏ​α

Magnetic moment due to spin¶

  • Analogously, the S^x\hat{S}_xS^x​ and S^y\hat{S}_yS^y​ operators can be defined. These do not commute with S^z\hat{S}_zS^z​. Because electrons have spin angular momentum, the unpaired electrons in silver atoms (Stern-Gerlach experiment) produce an overall magnetic moment (the “two spots” of silver atoms). The spin magnetic moment is proportional to the spin angular momentum:

μ^⃗S=−gee2meS^⃗{\vec{\hat{\mu}}_S = -\frac{g_e e}{2m_e}\vec{\hat{S}}}μ^​​S​=−2me​ge​e​S^
(35)

  • where geg_ege​ is the free-electron ggg-factor (2.002322 from quantum electrodynamics). The zzz-component of the spin magnetic moment is (zzz is the quantization axis):

Spin magnetic moment
μ^z=−gee2meS^z{\hat{\mu}_z = -\frac{g_e e}{2m_e}\hat{S}_z}μ^​z​=−2me​ge​e​S^z​
(36)

  • ge≈2g_e \approx 2ge​≈2 for electrons. This is explained by relativistic quantum theory.

The Hamiltonian depends on the spin operator¶

  • Following the eigenfunctions of SzS_zSz​ obtained above, the corresponding eigenvalues for the spin magnetic moment are:

μz=−geeℏ2mems=−geμBms{\mu_z = -\frac{g_e e\hbar}{2m_e}m_s = -g_e\mu_B m_s}μz​=−2me​ge​eℏ​ms​=−ge​μB​ms​
(37)

  • Thus the total energy for a spin in an external magnetic field is:

E=geμBmsB{E = g_e\mu_B m_s B}E=ge​μB​ms​B
(38)

  • where BBB is the magnetic field strength (in tesla). By combining the contributions from the hydrogenlike-atom Hamiltonian and the orbital and electron Zeeman terms, we get the total Hamiltonian:

H^=H^0+eB2meL^z+geeB2meS^z=H^0+eB2me(L^z+geS^z){\hat{H} = \hat{H}_0 + \frac{eB}{2m_e}\hat{L}_z + \frac{g_e eB}{2m_e}\hat{S}_z = \hat{H}_0 + \frac{eB}{2m_e}\left(\hat{L}_z + g_e\hat{S}_z\right)}H^=H^0​+2me​eB​L^z​+2me​ge​eB​S^z​=H^0​+2me​eB​(L^z​+ge​S^z​)
(39)

  • The eigenvalues of this operator are (derivation not shown):

En,ml,ms=En+eBℏ2me(ml+gems){E_{n,m_l,m_s} = E_n + \frac{eB\hbar}{2m_e}\left(m_l + g_e m_s\right)}En,ml​,ms​​=En​+2me​eBℏ​(ml​+ge​ms​)
(40)

Spin-orbit coupling¶

  • Having two sources of magnetic field in atoms, one due to orbital momentum and another due to spin, there arises the possibility that these microscopic magnets can interact.

  • This possibility is indeed realized and is known as spin-orbit coupling. A new term is added to the Hamiltonian to account for it:

H^=H^0+Ar3⋅L^⋅S^\hat{H} = \hat{H}_0 + \frac{A}{r^3} \cdot \hat{L}\cdot\hat{S}H^=H^0​+r3A​⋅L^⋅S^
(41)

  • where AAA is a constant, H^0\hat{H}_0H^0​ is the Hamiltonian for the H-atom without spin, and L^\hat{L}L^ and S^\hat{S}S^ are the orbital and spin angular momentum operators. Notice two things: first, the term decays as 1/r31/r^31/r3, faster than the potential energy, so this term makes a much smaller contribution than H^0\hat{H}_0H^0​.

  • Second, because of spin-orbit coupling, neither L^\hat{L}L^ nor S^\hat{S}S^ commutes with the Hamiltonian. Hence one needs a new quantum number JJJ to specify H-atom states. This new quantum number is the total angular momentum J⃗=L⃗+S⃗\vec{J} = \vec{L} + \vec{S}J=L+S.

Determining total angular momentum values for the H-atom¶

  1. Understand angular momentum coupling. In a hydrogen atom, the total angular momentum J⃗\vec{J}J is the vector sum of the orbital angular momentum L⃗\vec{L}L and the spin angular momentum S⃗\vec{S}S:

J⃗=L⃗+S⃗\vec{J} = \vec{L} + \vec{S}J=L+S
(42)

Here lll is the orbital angular momentum quantum number (l=0,1,2,…l = 0, 1, 2, \dotsl=0,1,2,…) and sss is the spin quantum number (s=12s = \frac{1}{2}s=21​ for an electron).

  1. Determine the possible jjj values. The quantum number jjj represents the magnitude of J⃗\vec{J}J and takes the values:

j=∣l−s∣,∣l−s∣+1,…,(l+s)j = |l - s|, |l - s| + 1, \dots, (l + s)j=∣l−s∣,∣l−s∣+1,…,(l+s)
(43)

  1. Calculate jjj based on lll and sss. Since s=12s = \frac{1}{2}s=21​ for a single electron:

  • If l=0l = 0l=0: j=12j = \frac{1}{2}j=21​ only.

  • If l=1l = 1l=1: j=12j = \frac{1}{2}j=21​ and j=32j = \frac{3}{2}j=23​.

  • If l=2l = 2l=2: j=32j = \frac{3}{2}j=23​ and j=52j = \frac{5}{2}j=25​.

  • In general: j=l±12j = l \pm \frac{1}{2}j=l±21​.

  1. Interpretation. These jjj values correspond to the quantized total angular momentum. The magnitude is ∣J⃗∣=j(j+1) ℏ|\vec{J}| = \sqrt{j(j+1)}\,\hbar∣J∣=j(j+1)​ℏ. The jjj values determine the energy-level splitting due to spin-orbit coupling (fine structure).

Term symbols¶

  • Because of spin-orbit coupling, the energy levels are no longer described by lll and sss separately. This is why one introduces term symbols, which encode the total spin multiplicity (2S+1)(2S+1)(2S+1), the angular momentum LLL, and the total angular momentum J=L+SJ = L + SJ=L+S. The word “total” takes on more meaning for multi-electron atoms, where angular momenta are summed. For the H-atom, S=1/2S = 1/2S=1/2 and L=lL = lL=l:

2S+1LJ^{2S+1}L_J2S+1LJ​
(44)
Example: microstates from spin-orbit coupling

Determine the microstates of l=1l = 1l=1 and l=2l = 2l=2 resulting from spin-orbit coupling.

Solution:

The j=l+sj = l + sj=l+s values follow the rules of spatial quantization of angular momentum, just like lll and sss: the projection of JJJ, namely mjm_jmj​, can take the values l+s,l+s−1,…,−(l+s)l+s, l+s-1, \ldots, -(l+s)l+s,l+s−1,…,−(l+s). Since s=1/2s = 1/2s=1/2, we get j=l+1/2j = l + 1/2j=l+1/2 and j=l−1/2j = l - 1/2j=l−1/2. The total number of microstates equals the 2 values of msm_sms​ (+1/2,−1/2+1/2, -1/2+1/2,−1/2) times the 2l+12l+12l+1 values of mlm_lml​, for a total of 2(2l+1)2(2l+1)2(2l+1) microstates.

For l=1l = 1l=1: the maximum projection is mj=1+1/2=3/2m_j = 1 + 1/2 = 3/2mj​=1+1/2=3/2, coming from j=3/2j = 3/2j=3/2; the minimum is mj=l−1/2=1/2m_j = l - 1/2 = 1/2mj​=l−1/2=1/2, coming from j=1/2j = 1/2j=1/2. We thus have two terms, 2P3/2^2P_{3/2}2P3/2​ and 2P1/2^2P_{1/2}2P1/2​.

The “anomalous” Zeeman effect¶

  • While the Zeeman effect in some atoms (e.g., hydrogen) showed the expected equally spaced triplet, in other atoms the magnetic field split the lines into four, six, or even more lines, with some spacings wider than expected. These deviations were labeled the anomalous Zeeman effect and were very puzzling to early researchers.

  • The explanation gave additional insight into electron spin. With the inclusion of electron spin in the total angular momentum, the other multiplets fall into a consistent picture. So what has historically been called the “anomalous” Zeeman effect is really the normal Zeeman effect once electron spin is included.

  • When a magnetic field is applied to the H-atom, the external field BBB now interacts with the total combined angular momentum JJJ, splitting the energy of each level with distinct JJJ. For example, 2P3/2^2P_{3/2}2P3/2​ splits into 2⋅3/2+1=42\cdot 3/2 + 1 = 42⋅3/2+1=4 lines and 2P1/2^2P_{1/2}2P1/2​ splits into 2⋅1/2+1=22\cdot 1/2 + 1 = 22⋅1/2+1=2 lines.

Selection rules for electronic transitions¶

  • Just as for other model systems, the probability of an electronic transition in the H-atom is given by the transition moment (or its zzz-projection), ⟨n′,l′,ml′,ms′∣z∣n,l,ml,ms⟩\langle n', l', m_l', m_s' \mid z \mid n, l, m_l, m_s \rangle⟨n′,l′,ml′​,ms′​∣z∣n,l,ml​,ms​⟩. Evaluating this expression using the properties of the special functions yields the following selection rules.

Selection rules for electronic transitions in the H-atom
ΔL=±1\Delta L = \pm 1ΔL=±1
(45)
ΔS=0\Delta S = 0ΔS=0
(46)
ΔJ=0,±1(0→0 forbidden)\Delta J = 0, \pm 1 \quad (0 \rightarrow 0 \text{ forbidden})ΔJ=0,±1(0→0 forbidden)
(47)

Summary of spin and angular momentum¶

  • Spin emerges naturally once one accounts for relativistic effects, as originally shown by Paul Dirac. Except in special cases, relativistic effects are not significant enough to include in quantum mechanics, so we incorporate spin as an additional degree of freedom that has not been accounted for but is known to exist.

Angular momentumSpin momentum
L^=r^×p^\hat{L} = \hat{r}\times \hat{p}L^=r^×p^​
L^z=−iℏ(x∂∂y−y∂∂x)\hat{L}_z = -i\hbar \left(x\frac{\partial}{\partial y} - y\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\right)L^z​=−iℏ(x∂y∂​−y∂x∂​)		S^\hat{S}S^
S^z\hat{S}_zS^z​
l=0,1,2,3,...l = 0, 1, 2, 3, ...l=0,1,2,3,...
ml=−l...0...lm_l = -l...0...lml​=−l...0...l		s=1/2s = 1/2s=1/2
ms=−1/2,1/2m_s = -1/2, 1/2ms​=−1/2,1/2
∣l,ml⟩=Yl,ml\mid l, m_l\rangle = Y_{l, m_l}∣l,ml​⟩=Yl,ml​​∣s,ms⟩=α,β\mid s, m_s\rangle = \alpha, \beta∣s,ms​⟩=α,β
∣1/2,+1/2⟩=α\mid 1/2, +1/2\rangle = \alpha∣1/2,+1/2⟩=α
∣1/2,−1/2⟩=β\mid 1/2, -1/2\rangle = \beta∣1/2,−1/2⟩=β
L=ℏl(l+1)L = \hbar\sqrt{l(l+1)}L=ℏl(l+1)​
Lz=ℏmlL_z = \hbar m_lLz​=ℏml​		S=ℏs(s+1)=ℏ3/4S = \hbar\sqrt{s(s+1)} = \hbar\sqrt{3/4}S=ℏs(s+1)​=ℏ3/4​
Sz=ℏms=±ℏ/2S_z = \hbar m_s = \pm \hbar/2Sz​=ℏms​=±ℏ/2
μL=−gle2meL\mu_L = -g_l \frac{e}{2m_e}LμL​=−gl​2me​e​L
gl=1g_l = 1gl​=1		μS=gse2meS\mu_S = g_s \frac{e}{2m_e}SμS​=gs​2me​e​S
gs≈2g_s \approx 2gs​≈2

Problems¶

These problems span the whole chapter, from hydrogen orbitals and their quantum numbers to electron spin, Zeeman splitting, and spin-orbit coupling.

Problem 1: Counting states for n=3n = 3n=3

  • For the hydrogen atom, how many possible quantum states correspond to the principal quantum number n=3n = 3n=3?

  • What are the energies of these states?

  • Consider several values of nnn and show that the number of orbitals for each nnn is n2n^2n2.

Solution:

If n=3n = 3n=3, the allowed values of lll are 0, 1, and 2.

  • If l=0l = 0l=0, then m=0m = 0m=0 (1 state).

  • If l=1l = 1l=1, then m=−1,0,1m = -1, 0, 1m=−1,0,1 (3 states).

  • If l=2l = 2l=2, then m=−2,−1,0,1,2m = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2m=−2,−1,0,1,2 (5 states).

In total there are 1+3+5=91 + 3 + 5 = 91+3+5=9 allowed states. This confirms that the number of orbitals for the H-atom is n2n^2n2.

Because the total energy depends only on the principal quantum number n=3n = 3n=3, the energy of each of these states is:

En=−13.6⋅1n2 [eV]E_n = -13.6 \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} \, [\text{eV}]En​=−13.6⋅n21​[eV]
(48)
Problem 2: Nodes of the 3pz3p_z3pz​ orbital

Locate the nodes (and nodal surfaces) of the 3pz3p_z3pz​ orbital:

ψ3pz=1812π(1a0)3/2(6ra0−r2a02)e−r/3a0cos⁡(θ)\psi_{3p_z} = \frac{1}{81}\sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}}\left(\frac{1}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}\left(6\frac{r}{a_0} - \frac{r^2}{a_0^2}\right) e^{-r/3a_0}\cos(\theta)ψ3pz​​=811​π2​​(a0​1​)3/2(6a0​r​−a02​r2​)e−r/3a0​cos(θ)
(49)
Solution:

  • 3pz3p_z3pz​ corresponds to n=3n = 3n=3, l=1l = 1l=1, and ml=0m_l = 0ml​=0.

  • The radial part is zero at r=0r = 0r=0 and r=6a0r = 6a_0r=6a0​. The first is part of the boundary condition and not a node, because the wavefunction does not change sign there. Hence there is 1 radial node.

  • The angular part has one nodal surface from cos⁡θ=0\cos\theta = 0cosθ=0, which occurs at θ=π2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}θ=2π​ (the xyxyxy-plane).

  • We thus have 2 nodes for the 3pz3p_z3pz​ orbital. The total number of nodes is n−1n - 1n−1, made up of lll angular nodes and n−l−1n - l - 1n−l−1 radial nodes.

Problem 3: The four quantum numbers of the H-atom

  • Why do we use four quantum numbers nnn, lll, mlm_lml​, msm_sms​ to specify H-atom states such as ∣1,0,0,+1/2⟩|1, 0, 0, +1/2\rangle∣1,0,0,+1/2⟩?

  • What can we say about the operators whose eigenvalues are defined by these quantum numbers?

Solution:

  • These four quantum numbers uniquely specify the state of the H-atom (including spin). They are the labels of a complete set of commuting observables, so they can all be measured simultaneously.

  • The operators H^\hat{H}H^, L^2\hat{L}^2L^2, L^z\hat{L}_zL^z​, and S^z\hat{S}_zS^z​ all commute with one another, which is precisely why a single state can carry definite values nnn, lll, mlm_lml​, and msm_sms​.

Problem 4: Microstates of an l=4l = 4l=4 electron

Determine the term symbols (microstates) of the H-atom corresponding to l=4l = 4l=4.

Solution:

For l=4l = 4l=4 (ggg electron) and s=1/2s = 1/2s=1/2, the total angular momentum takes the values j=l±12=92j = l \pm \frac{1}{2} = \frac{9}{2}j=l±21​=29​ and 72\frac{7}{2}27​. The spin multiplicity is 2S+1=22S + 1 = 22S+1=2 and the orbital letter for l=4l = 4l=4 is GGG. The two term symbols are therefore 2G9/2^2G_{9/2}2G9/2​ and 2G7/2^2G_{7/2}2G7/2​. The total number of microstates is 2(2l+1)=2×9=182(2l+1) = 2 \times 9 = 182(2l+1)=2×9=18, distributed as (2j+1)=10(2j+1) = 10(2j+1)=10 states for j=9/2j = 9/2j=9/2 and 8 states for j=7/2j = 7/2j=7/2.

Problem 5: Zeeman splitting of a 2p2p2p electron

A hydrogen atom in a 2p2p2p state is placed in a magnetic field of B=2.0B = 2.0B=2.0 T. Ignoring spin, into how many levels does the orbital Zeeman effect split the 2p2p2p level, and what is the energy spacing between adjacent levels?

Problem 6: Magnitude versus projection of spin

For an electron, compute the magnitude ∣S⃗∣|\vec{S}|∣S∣ of the spin angular momentum and the allowed projections SzS_zSz​. Explain why ∣S⃗∣|\vec{S}|∣S∣ is larger than the maximum value of SzS_zSz​.

Problem 7: Spin-orbit and the sodium doublet

Sodium’s bright yellow emission is a closely spaced doublet (the DDD lines) arising from the 3p→3s3p \rightarrow 3s3p→3s transition. Using the selection rules and the term symbols of a single ppp electron, explain why this line is a doublet rather than a single line.

Problem 8: Average potential energy of the H-atom

Calculate the average potential energy of the hydrogen atom in its ground and first excited states, using ⟨V⟩=−Ze24πϵ0⟨r−1⟩\langle V \rangle = -\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\langle r^{-1}\rangle⟨V⟩=−4πϵ0​Ze2​⟨r−1⟩ with ⟨r−1⟩n=Za0n2\langle r^{-1}\rangle_{n} = \frac{Z}{a_0 n^2}⟨r−1⟩n​=a0​n2Z​. Show that your result satisfies the virial relation ⟨V⟩=2En\langle V \rangle = 2E_n⟨V⟩=2En​.
